docs.title: Can machines learn to classify patterns from labeled examples?
docs.body:
The foundational question of whether a mechanical system could learn
input-output mappings from examples, rather than being explicitly
programmed with rules. This was the core premise of the perceptron
and the starting point for all supervised learning.

docs.title: Can perceptrons solve non-linearly separable problems like XOR?
docs.body:
Minsky and Papert showed that single-layer perceptrons cannot represent
XOR or other non-linearly separable functions. This raised the question
of whether neural networks were fundamentally limited.

docs.title: Can multi-layer neural networks be trained effectively with gradient descent?
docs.body:
After Minsky and Papert showed single-layer limits, the key question became
whether deeper networks (which theoretically could solve non-linear problems)
could actually be trained. The credit assignment problem — how to update
weights in hidden layers — was the core challenge.

docs.title: Can neural networks learn long-range temporal dependencies in sequences?
docs.body:
Standard recurrent neural networks suffer from vanishing and exploding
gradients, making it impossible to learn dependencies across many time
steps. This was a fundamental barrier for sequence modeling.

docs.title: Can neural networks learn hierarchical feature representations directly from raw data?
docs.body:
The fundamental question of whether deep architectures could automatically
discover increasingly abstract features without hand-engineered feature
extractors. This challenged the prevailing approach of manual feature
engineering that dominated computer vision and NLP through the 2000s.

docs.title: Can neural networks generate realistic, novel images?
docs.body:
Whether neural networks could move beyond classification to actually
generate new data — images that look real but don't exist in the
training set. This required learning the underlying data distribution.

docs.title: Can very deep networks be trained without performance degradation?
docs.body:
As networks got deeper beyond ~20 layers, training accuracy actually
decreased — not from overfitting but from optimization difficulty.
Simply stacking more layers did not improve performance.

docs.title: Can AI achieve superhuman performance in complex strategic games through self-play?
docs.body:
Go was considered a grand challenge for AI due to its enormous search
space (10^170 positions). Traditional game tree search was insufficient.
Could neural networks combined with reinforcement learning master it?

docs.title: Is there a more parallelizable alternative to RNNs for sequence modeling?
docs.body:
RNNs (including LSTMs) process sequences step-by-step, making them
inherently sequential and slow to train. Could an architecture process
all positions simultaneously while still capturing long-range dependencies?

docs.title: Can unsupervised pre-training on text transfer effectively to all NLP tasks?
docs.body:
Traditional NLP required task-specific architectures and labeled data for
each problem. Could a single model pre-trained on unlabeled text learn
general language understanding transferable to any downstream task?

docs.title: Can AI learn accurate internal world models from observation alone?
docs.body:
Do large models trained on text and images develop genuine internal
representations of the world, or are they sophisticated pattern matchers?
Building accurate world models is considered essential for robust
planning and reasoning.

docs.title: Do neural language model capabilities scale predictably with compute, data, and parameters?
docs.body:
As models grew larger, it was unclear whether improvements would continue
or hit diminishing returns. Understanding the relationship between scale
and performance was critical for resource allocation.

docs.title: Is there a unified framework for continuous (intuitive) and discrete (symbolic) reasoning?
docs.body:
Neural networks excel at pattern recognition (System 1) but struggle with
multi-step logical reasoning (System 2). Symbolic systems have the opposite
profile. Can we unify both in a single architecture?

docs.title: What are the optimal compute-data trade-offs for training large language models?
docs.body:
Given a fixed compute budget, should you train a larger model on less data
or a smaller model on more data? Getting this wrong wastes millions in
compute. Kaplan et al. suggested larger models, but was that right?

docs.title: Can large language models be aligned to reliably follow human intent?
docs.body:
Raw language models generate text that is probabilistically likely but not
necessarily helpful, harmless, or honest. Could human feedback be used
to steer model behavior toward human preferences?

docs.title: Can foundation models achieve robust out-of-distribution generalization?
docs.body:
Current models perform well on data similar to their training distribution
but can fail unpredictably on novel situations. Can we build models that
generalize robustly to truly new domains and edge cases?

docs.title: Can AI generate high-quality, diverse images from text descriptions?
docs.body:
Going beyond unconditional image generation — could models understand
natural language descriptions and produce corresponding images with
high fidelity and creative diversity?

docs.title: Can language models learn to use external tools to overcome their inherent limitations?
docs.body:
LLMs are limited by their training data cutoff, inability to do precise
math, and lack of real-time information. Could they learn to call APIs,
search engines, and calculators to augment their capabilities?

docs.title: Can alignment techniques scale to superintelligent AI systems?
docs.body:
RLHF works for current models, but as models become more capable than
their human evaluators, how can we ensure they remain aligned? The
fundamental challenge is that humans may not be able to evaluate
superhuman outputs.

docs.title: Can AI systems reliably detect and correct their own reasoning errors?
docs.body:
LLMs are prone to hallucination and logical errors. Can models develop
reliable self-evaluation capabilities, recognizing when they are wrong
and correcting themselves without external feedback?

docs.title: The Perceptron: A Probabilistic Model for Information Storage and Organization in the Brain
docs.body:
The perceptron proved machines could learn pattern classification
from labeled examples, launching the field of neural network research.
 A single-layer neural network can learn to classify linearly separable
patterns from labeled examples using a simple weight update rule.
 The perceptron convergence theorem guarantees that the algorithm will
find a solution if one exists in finite steps.

docs.title: Perceptrons: An Introduction to Computational Geometry
docs.body:
Proved single-layer perceptrons cannot solve non-linear problems,
contributing to the first AI winter but also motivating multi-layer research.
 Single-layer perceptrons cannot compute XOR or any non-linearly
separable function. They are limited to linear decision boundaries.

docs.title: Learning representations by back-propagating errors
docs.body:
Backpropagation made multi-layer neural networks trainable, solving the
credit assignment problem and launching the connectionism revival.
 The backpropagation algorithm can efficiently compute gradients for all
weights in a multi-layer network using the chain rule, enabling training
of networks with hidden layers.
 Multi-layer networks trained with backprop can learn distributed
representations that capture abstract features of the input.
docs.authors: David Rumelhart, Geoffrey Hinton, Ronald Williams

docs.title: Long Short-Term Memory
docs.body:
LSTM solved the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs with gated memory
cells, enabling learning of long-range dependencies in sequences.
 The LSTM architecture uses gated memory cells to selectively remember
and forget information, solving the vanishing gradient problem for
recurrent neural networks.

docs.title: ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
docs.body:
AlexNet proved deep CNNs learn hierarchical visual representations far
superior to hand-engineered features, launching the deep learning revolution.
 A deep CNN trained end-to-end on ImageNet achieved a top-5 error rate
of 15.3%, dramatically outperforming the second-place hand-engineered
approach (26.2%).
 ReLU activations and GPU training enabled practical training of
deep convolutional networks on large datasets.
docs.authors: Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever, Geoffrey Hinton

docs.title: Generative Adversarial Nets
docs.body:
GANs proved neural networks can generate realistic novel images through
adversarial training, creating a new paradigm for generative modeling.
 A generator and discriminator network trained adversarially can learn
to produce realistic samples from the data distribution without
explicit density estimation.
docs.authors: Ian Goodfellow, Jean Pouget-Abadie, Mehdi Mirza, Bing Xu, David Warde-Farley

docs.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
docs.body:
ResNet introduced skip connections enabling training of 100+ layer networks,
solving the depth degradation problem and becoming a ubiquitous building block.
 Skip connections (residual connections) allow training of networks
with 152+ layers by letting gradients flow directly through shortcut
paths, eliminating the degradation problem.
docs.authors: Kaiming He, Xiangyu Zhang, Shaoqing Ren, Jian Sun

docs.title: Mastering the game of Go with deep neural networks and tree search
docs.body:
AlphaGo defeated the world Go champion by combining deep neural networks
with tree search, proving AI can master complex strategic reasoning.
 AlphaGo combined deep neural networks (policy and value networks) with
Monte Carlo tree search to defeat the world champion at Go, a game
previously considered decades away from AI mastery.
docs.authors: David Silver, Aja Huang, Chris Maddison, Arthur Guez, Laurent Sifre

docs.title: Attention Is All You Need
docs.body:
The Transformer replaced RNNs with self-attention for fully parallel
sequence processing, becoming the foundation for all modern large language models.
 The Transformer architecture uses self-attention to process all positions
in parallel, replacing recurrence entirely while achieving state-of-the-art
translation quality.
 Multi-head attention allows the model to jointly attend to information
from different representation subspaces at different positions.
docs.authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones

docs.title: Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training
docs.body:
GPT demonstrated that generative pre-training on unlabeled text produces
transferable language representations, launching the foundation model era.
 A Transformer trained with autoregressive language modeling on a large
unlabeled corpus learns representations that transfer to diverse NLP
tasks with minimal fine-tuning.
docs.authors: Alec Radford, Karthik Narasimhan, Tim Salimans, Ilya Sutskever

docs.title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
docs.body:
BERT showed bidirectional pre-training produces superior language
understanding, making a single model competitive across all NLP benchmarks.
 Bidirectional pre-training with masked language modeling achieves
state-of-the-art on 11 NLP benchmarks simultaneously, surpassing
task-specific architectures.
docs.authors: Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova

docs.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
docs.body:
Established that language model performance follows predictable power laws
with scale, making the case for dramatically larger models.
 Language model performance follows smooth power-law relationships with
model size, dataset size, and compute budget, spanning many orders of
magnitude with remarkable predictability.
 Performance depends most strongly on scale (compute, data, parameters)
and is surprisingly insensitive to architectural details within the
Transformer family.
docs.authors: Jared Kaplan, Sam McCandlish, Tom Henighan, Tom Brown, Benjamin Chess

docs.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback
docs.body:
InstructGPT showed RLHF can align LLMs to human intent, making a small
aligned model preferred over a much larger unaligned one.
 RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) using a reward model
trained on human preference comparisons dramatically improves instruction
following. A 1.3B InstructGPT model was preferred over a 175B GPT-3.
docs.authors: Long Ouyang, Jeffrey Wu, Xu Jiang, Diogo Almeida, Carroll Wainwright

docs.title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models
docs.body:
Chinchilla established that models should be trained on far more data than
previously assumed, rebalancing the compute-optimal frontier for LLMs.
 Current large language models are significantly over-parameterized and
under-trained. For compute-optimal training, model size and training
data should be scaled equally.
docs.authors: Jordan Hoffmann, Sebastian Borgeaud, Arthur Mensch, Elena Buchatskaya, Trevor Cai

docs.title: Hierarchical Text-Conditional Image Generation with CLIP Latents
docs.body:
DALL-E 2 demonstrated that diffusion models conditioned on CLIP embeddings
can generate high-quality images from text, making AI art commercially viable.
 Combining CLIP text-image embeddings with diffusion models enables
generation of highly realistic and diverse images from natural language
descriptions, with fine-grained control over composition and style.
docs.authors: Aditya Ramesh, Prafulla Dhariwal, Alex Nichol, Casey Chu, Mark Chen

docs.title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
docs.body:
Toolformer proved LLMs can self-teach tool use, enabling them to overcome
inherent limitations by calling external APIs when needed.
 Language models can learn to use external tools (calculator, search
engine, translation API) in a self-supervised way, deciding when and
how to call tools by generating special API call tokens.
docs.authors: Timo Schick, Jane Dwivedi-Yu, Roberto Dessi, Roberta Raileanu, Maria Lomeli

docs.title: Learning to Reason with LLMs (OpenAI o1)
docs.body:
Chain-of-thought reasoning at test time, trained via reinforcement learning,
enables models to catch and correct errors during extended reasoning chains.
o1 significantly outperforms GPT-4 on math, coding, and science benchmarks.

docs.title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
docs.body:
Models can critique and revise their own outputs according to a set of
constitutional principles, reducing dependence on human labelers for
alignment while maintaining helpfulness.
